name: New Zealand
summary: New Zealand is a cool maritime wine country known for intensely aromatic wines with high acidity, especially Sauvignon Blanc.
examTips: New Zealand is strongly linked with cool maritime climate.
Sauvignon Blanc is the key grape.
Think high acidity and very pronounced aromatics.

marlborough.name: Marlborough
marlborough.summary: New Zealand’s most important region, famous for intensely aromatic Sauvignon Blanc.
marlborough.styleSummary: Very aromatic wines with high acidity.
examTips: This is the most important New Zealand region.
Extremely aromatic style is key.
Think gooseberry, passion fruit, and herbaceous notes.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: World-famous aromatic white producing intense citrus, tropical and herbaceous notes with high acidity.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: Gooseberry, passion fruit, citrus, herbaceous, high acidity.
examTips: Very pronounced aromatics is the key clue.
More intense than most Old World Sauvignon Blanc.

premium-sparkling.name: Premium Sparkling Wine
premium-sparkling.style: Traditional method sparkling wine with high acidity, citrus fruit and fresh autolytic notes.
premium-sparkling.keyPoint: Marlborough is the key New Zealand sparkling region in the Level 3 syllabus.

wairau-valley.name: Wairau Valley
wairau-valley.summary: Broad warmer Marlborough valley producing the classic pungent passion-fruit style of Sauvignon Blanc.
wairau-valley.styleSummary: Pronounced Sauvignon Blanc with passion fruit, gooseberry and high acidity.
examTips: Wairau is the benchmark Marlborough expression many students picture first.
Expect intense tropical and herbaceous character.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: World-famous aromatic white producing intense citrus, tropical and herbaceous notes with high acidity.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: Gooseberry, passion fruit, citrus, herbaceous, high acidity.
examTips: Very pronounced aromatics is the key clue.
More intense than most Old World Sauvignon Blanc.

wairau-valley.classification: Benchmark Marlborough valley for the classic passion-fruit style.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

awatere-valley.name: Awatere Valley
awatere-valley.summary: Cooler windier Marlborough valley producing more restrained and herbaceous Sauvignon Blanc.
awatere-valley.styleSummary: More pungent herbal citrus style with very high acidity.
examTips: Usually cooler and more herbal than Wairau.
High acidity and pungency are classic clues.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: World-famous aromatic white producing intense citrus, tropical and herbaceous notes with high acidity.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: Gooseberry, passion fruit, citrus, herbaceous, high acidity.
examTips: Very pronounced aromatics is the key clue.
More intense than most Old World Sauvignon Blanc.

awatere-valley.classification: Cooler windier valley often giving more pungent herbal Sauvignon Blanc.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

marlborough.classification: Marlborough is the flagship New Zealand GI for Sauvignon Blanc, with valley differences important for style.
lawNotes: Wairau is often broader and more tropical, while Awatere is usually cooler and more herbal.
High acidity and pungent aromatics are central exam clues.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Choice of production method and maturation is a major human factor in shaping regional wine style.

hawkes-bay.name: Hawke’s Bay
hawkes-bay.summary: Warmer North Island region producing fuller-bodied reds and richer whites.
hawkes-bay.styleSummary: Riper wines with more body and structure.
examTips: Warmer than Marlborough.
Important for Bordeaux-style blends.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Structured red grape producing blackcurrant and cassis flavours.
examTips: Usually blended with Merlot.

Merlot.summary: Soft red grape producing plum and black fruit wines.
examTips: Softer than Cabernet.

Chardonnay.summary: Fuller-bodied Chardonnay with stone fruit and possible oak influence.
examTips: Richer than Marlborough styles.

gimblett-gravels.name: Gimblett Gravels
gimblett-gravels.summary: Warm stony Hawke's Bay sub-region especially associated with Bordeaux blends and Syrah.
gimblett-gravels.styleSummary: Riper fuller-bodied reds with structured tannin and concentrated fruit.
examTips: Warm gravel soils are central here.
A key premium New Zealand red wine name.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Structured red grape producing blackcurrant and cassis flavours.
examTips: Usually blended with Merlot.

Merlot.summary: Soft red grape producing plum and black fruit wines.
examTips: Softer than Cabernet.

Chardonnay.summary: Fuller-bodied Chardonnay with stone fruit and possible oak influence.
examTips: Richer than Marlborough styles.

gimblett-gravels.classification: Prestige stony warm sub-region associated with premium red wines.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

hawkes-bay.classification: Hawke’s Bay is a warm New Zealand region especially important for Bordeaux blends, Syrah and some Chardonnay.
lawNotes: Gimblett Gravels is a key premium sub-region name.
Warmer conditions contrast clearly with Marlborough and Central Otago.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Regional style depends not only on climate and site, but also on deliberate human choices in the vineyard and cellar.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

central-otago.name: Central Otago
central-otago.summary: Cool southern region known for high-quality Pinot Noir.
examTips: Cool climate Pinot Noir.
Think purity of fruit.

Pinot Noir.summary: Elegant red grape producing bright red fruit wines with fresh acidity.
Pinot Noir.style: Red cherry, raspberry, light to medium body.
examTips: More fruit-driven than Burgundy.

bannockburn.name: Bannockburn
bannockburn.summary: Prestige Central Otago sub-region known for concentrated Pinot Noir from a dry continental climate.
bannockburn.styleSummary: Ripe yet fresh Pinot Noir with red and black cherry fruit and bright acidity.
examTips: Central Otago is New Zealand's most continental major region.
Pinot Noir is the key grape.

Pinot Noir.summary: Elegant red grape producing bright red fruit wines with fresh acidity.
Pinot Noir.style: Red cherry, raspberry, light to medium body.
examTips: More fruit-driven than Burgundy.

bannockburn.classification: Warm prestige Central Otago sub-region known for concentrated Pinot Noir.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Subregional site differences are important to how grapes ripen and to the final balance of the wines.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

gibbston.name: Gibbston
gibbston.summary: Cooler elevated Central Otago sub-region producing lighter fresher Pinot Noir.
gibbston.styleSummary: More delicate Pinot Noir with bright acidity and floral lift.
examTips: Higher altitude and cooler conditions mean a lighter style than the warmest Central Otago sites.
Still unmistakably Pinot-focused.

Pinot Noir.summary: Elegant red grape producing bright red fruit wines with fresh acidity.
Pinot Noir.style: Red cherry, raspberry, light to medium body.
examTips: More fruit-driven than Burgundy.

gibbston.classification: Cooler higher sub-region generally associated with a lighter fresher style.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.

Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

central-otago.classification: Central Otago is New Zealand’s most continental major region and the key premium Pinot Noir area.
lawNotes: A continental climate and high UV at altitude are important context clues.
Pinot Noir is the defining grape.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.

Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

wairarapa.name: Wairarapa (Martinborough)
wairarapa.summary: Small region producing premium Pinot Noir with structure and complexity.
wairarapa.styleSummary: Structured Pinot Noir with more concentration.
examTips: More structured than Central Otago.
Smaller but premium region.

Pinot Noir.summary: More structured Pinot Noir with deeper fruit concentration.
examTips: Compare with Central Otago.

martinborough.name: Martinborough
martinborough.summary: Prestige Wairarapa area especially associated with premium Pinot Noir.
martinborough.styleSummary: Elegant Pinot Noir with red fruit, spice and fresh acidity.
examTips: Martinborough is the key name inside Wairarapa to remember.
Pinot Noir is the defining grape.

Pinot Noir.summary: More structured Pinot Noir with deeper fruit concentration.
examTips: Compare with Central Otago.

martinborough.classification: The key named Wairarapa premium Pinot Noir area.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Wind can cool vineyards and reduce fungal disease pressure, but can also affect vigor and fruit set.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Training systems, row orientation, and site protection matter where wind is a major factor.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.
